name: Music
description: Explore genres, legendary artists, instruments, and music history.

questions.text: How many strings does a standard guitar have?
questions.explanation: A standard guitar has 6 strings, tuned E-A-D-G-B-E from lowest to highest.

questions.text: Which band is known for the song 'Bohemian Rhapsody'?
questions.explanation: Bohemian Rhapsody was written by Freddie Mercury and recorded by Queen, released in 1975.

questions.text: What is the term for the speed of a piece of music?
questions.explanation: Tempo refers to the speed or pace of a piece of music, often measured in beats per minute (BPM).

questions.text: Which artist is known as the 'King of Pop'?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson earned the title 'King of Pop' due to his massive global influence on popular music.

questions.text: How many keys does a standard piano have?
questions.explanation: A standard piano has 88 keys — 52 white keys and 36 black keys.

questions.text: What is the highest-pitched member of the violin family?
questions.explanation: The violin is the highest-pitched instrument in the violin family, which includes viola, cello, and double bass.

questions.text: Which music genre originated in Jamaica in the 1960s?
questions.explanation: Reggae originated in Jamaica in the late 1960s, strongly associated with artist Bob Marley.

questions.text: What does 'forte' mean in musical notation?
questions.explanation: Forte (marked 'f') is an Italian musical term meaning loud or strong.

questions.text: Which famous composer was deaf when he wrote his 9th Symphony?
questions.explanation: Ludwig van Beethoven was completely deaf when he composed his Ninth Symphony, premiered in 1824.

questions.text: How many notes are in a standard Western musical scale (octave)?
questions.explanation: A standard Western musical scale (e.g., C major) contains 8 notes spanning an octave.

questions.text: Which band released the album 'Abbey Road' in 1969?
questions.explanation: Abbey Road is the eleventh studio album by The Beatles, released in September 1969.

questions.text: What is the name of the symbol that indicates a note should be played softly?
questions.explanation: Piano (marked 'p') is the musical notation symbol for soft or quiet playing.

questions.text: Which singer is known for hits like 'Rolling in the Deep' and 'Hello'?
questions.explanation: Adele is a British singer-songwriter known for chart-topping hits like Rolling in the Deep and Hello.

questions.text: What type of instrument is a trumpet?
questions.explanation: A trumpet is a brass instrument; sound is produced by buzzing the lips into a metal mouthpiece.

questions.text: Which music streaming platform launched in 2008 in Sweden?
questions.explanation: Spotify launched in October 2008 and was founded in Stockholm, Sweden.

questions.text: What does BPM stand for in music?
questions.explanation: BPM stands for Beats Per Minute, the standard measure of tempo in music.

questions.text: Who is often called the 'Queen of Soul'?
questions.explanation: Aretha Franklin earned the title 'Queen of Soul' for her powerful voice and influence on R&B and soul music.

questions.text: Which genre of music is characterized by a 12-bar chord progression and originated from African American communities in the Deep South?
questions.explanation: Blues music is defined by its 12-bar structure and originated from African American communities in the Mississippi Delta.

questions.text: How many movements does a traditional symphony typically have?
questions.explanation: A traditional symphony typically has 4 movements, each with a different tempo and character.

questions.text: Which composer wrote 'The Four Seasons'?
questions.explanation: Antonio Vivaldi composed The Four Seasons, a group of four violin concertos, around 1721.

questions.text: What is a 'capella' or 'a cappella' singing?
questions.explanation: A cappella refers to vocal music performed without any instrumental accompaniment.

questions.text: In which decade did hip-hop music emerge as a genre?
questions.explanation: Hip-hop emerged in the 1970s in the South Bronx, New York, pioneered by artists like DJ Kool Herc.

questions.text: What is the name of Taylor Swift's debut album?
questions.explanation: Taylor Swift's self-titled debut album was released in October 2006.

questions.text: Which musical term means to play notes in a smooth, connected manner?
questions.explanation: Legato is the musical term for playing notes in a smooth, flowing, connected manner.

questions.text: Which artist holds the record for the best-selling album of all time?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson's Thriller is widely regarded as the best-selling album of all time, with estimated sales of over 66 million copies.

questions.text: What is the name of the musical note that receives one beat in 4/4 time?
questions.explanation: In 4/4 time, the quarter note (crotchet) receives one beat, with four beats per measure.

questions.text: Which rock band is known as 'The Fab Four'?
questions.explanation: The Beatles are known as 'The Fab Four,' referring to members John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr.

questions.text: What is the lowest-pitched member of the woodwind family commonly used in orchestras?
questions.explanation: The bassoon is the lowest-pitched member of the woodwind family used in a standard orchestra.

questions.text: Which famous music venue in Nashville is known as 'The Mother Church of Country Music'?
questions.explanation: The Ryman Auditorium in Nashville is called 'The Mother Church of Country Music' and served as the original home of the Grand Ole Opry.

questions.text: In music notation, what does a 'fermata' symbol indicate?
options: Hold the note longer than its written value
questions.explanation: A fermata (the 'bird's eye' symbol) tells a performer to hold a note or rest longer than its written value, at the performer's discretion.

questions.text: Which country is Flamenco music and dance traditionally associated with?
questions.explanation: Flamenco originated in Andalusia, Spain, and is a deeply rooted part of Spanish culture.

questions.text: What year did Elvis Presley release 'Hound Dog'?
questions.explanation: Elvis Presley released 'Hound Dog' in 1956, and it became one of his signature songs.

questions.text: Which instrument does a 'drummer' play in a jazz trio context?
questions.explanation: In a jazz trio, the typical lineup includes piano, upright bass, and drum kit.

questions.text: What type of voice is lower than a soprano but higher than an alto?
questions.explanation: A mezzo-soprano voice sits between soprano (highest female voice) and alto (lower female voice).

questions.text: Which musician is known as 'Slowhand'?
questions.explanation: Eric Clapton is nicknamed 'Slowhand,' a name that originated from his habit of re-stringing guitar strings during performances.

questions.text: What interval separates a note and its octave?
questions.explanation: An octave spans exactly 12 semitones in equal temperament, doubling the frequency of the original note.

questions.text: Which composer wrote 'The Rite of Spring,' whose 1913 premiere famously caused a riot in the audience?
questions.explanation: Igor Stravinsky's The Rite of Spring premiered in Paris in 1913 and its radical music and choreography caused an audience riot.

questions.text: What is the term for the technique of plucking violin strings instead of bowing them?
questions.explanation: Pizzicato is the technique of plucking string instrument strings with the fingers rather than using a bow.

questions.text: In music theory, what is an 'enharmonic equivalent'?
options: A note played at double the speed
Two notes that sound the same but are written differently
A chord played in a different key
questions.explanation: Enharmonic equivalents are notes that sound identical in pitch but are written differently, such as C# and Db.

questions.text: Which time signature is commonly referred to as 'common time'?
questions.explanation: 4/4 time is called 'common time' because it is the most frequently used time signature in Western music.

questions.text: Which composer is associated with the development of the 'twelve-tone technique'?
questions.explanation: Arnold Schoenberg developed the twelve-tone (dodecaphonic) technique in the early 1920s.

questions.text: What is 'serialism' in 20th-century classical music?
options: Music played in a series of concerts
A method using an ordered sequence of pitches to organize a composition
Music written in a series of keys
questions.explanation: Serialism is a compositional technique using an ordered sequence (series) of pitches or other elements to structure a piece.

questions.text: In what key does the standard guitar tuning have its lowest open string?
questions.explanation: Standard guitar tuning has the lowest (6th) string tuned to E2.

questions.text: What is the name of the phenomenon where two notes played together create a third, phantom tone?
questions.explanation: A combination tone (also called a Tartini tone) is a phantom pitch perceived when two loud tones are played simultaneously.

questions.text: Which avant-garde composer created '4'33"', a piece consisting of four minutes and 33 seconds of silence?
questions.explanation: John Cage composed 4'33" in 1952, directing the performer to not intentionally make any sounds for its duration.

questions.text: What does 'counterpoint' refer to in music theory?
options: The relationship between two or more melodic lines played simultaneously
The lowest note in a chord
questions.explanation: Counterpoint is the technique of combining two or more independent melodic lines that sound harmonious when played simultaneously.

questions.text: Which jazz legend is known as the 'Father of Bebop'?
questions.explanation: Charlie Parker (nicknamed 'Bird') is widely regarded as the 'Father of Bebop' for pioneering the modern jazz style.

questions.text: In electronic music, what is an 'LFO'?
questions.explanation: LFO stands for Low Frequency Oscillator, a component used in synthesizers to create modulation effects like vibrato and tremolo.

questions.text: Which musical concept describes the tonal center or 'home key' of a piece?
questions.explanation: Tonality refers to the system of organizing music around a central tonal center or tonic note (home key).

questions.text: What is a 'Neapolitan chord' in Western harmony?
options: A chord from Italian folk music
A major chord built on the flattened second scale degree
A seventh chord played in root position
A chord with an added ninth
questions.explanation: A Neapolitan chord is a major chord built on the flattened second (bII) scale degree, commonly used in classical and romantic music.
